title: To amend the Act of August 9, 1955 (commonly known as the “Long-Term Leasing Act”), to authorize leases of up to 99 years for land in the Mashpee Wampanoag Tribe Reservation and land held in trust for the Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head (Aquinnah), and for other purposes
plain_summary: This bill changes an old law from 1955 to give two Native American tribes in Massachusetts new powers over their land. The Mashpee Wampanoag Tribe and the Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head can now make rental agreements for their reservation and trust lands that last up to 99 years. Before this change, these tribes could not make such long rental deals with their land. The bill adds these two tribes to a list of other groups that already had permission to make these long-term land leases.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Indian Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1  Mashpee Wampanoag Tribe and Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head (Aquinnah) Leasing Authority
sections.text: This section changes an existing law from nineteen fifty-five called the Long-Term Leasing Act. The change adds two new groups to the list of tribes that can make long-term leases on their land. These two groups are the Mashpee Wampanoag Tribe Reservation and land held in trust for the Wampanoag Tribe of Gay Head which is also called Aquinnah. A lease is an agreement that lets someone use land for a certain amount of time in exchange for payment. Trust land means the federal government holds the land for the benefit of the tribe. By adding these tribes to the law, they will be able to make leases that last up to ninety-nine years on their reservation and trust lands.
